@@ -132,7 +132,7 @@ {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-db0","title":"Implement shell tool updates (omz, p10k, plugins, atuin, zoxide)","description":"## What Implement the 'shell' category for acfs-update: - Oh-My-Zsh: Run $ZSH/tools/upgrade.sh (non-interactive) - Powerlevel10k: git pull --ff-only in $ZSH_CUSTOM/themes/powerlevel10k - zsh-autosuggestions: git pull --ff-only in plugin dir - zsh-syntax-highlighting: git pull --ff-only in plugin dir - Atuin: Use atuin self-update if available, else reinstall - Zoxide: Reinstall via official installer ## Git-Based Updates (omz, p10k, plugins) - Use git pull --ff-only to avoid merge conflicts - If fast-forward fails: warn and skip (user has local changes) - OMZ has its own upgrade script that handles prompts ## Installer-Based Updates (Atuin, Zoxide) These use curl|sh installers like the stack tools: 1. Atuin: Try `atuin self-update` first (if available) - If no self-update: fetch installer, verify checksum, run 2. Zoxide: Fetch installer, verify checksum against checksums.yaml, run For checksum mismatches: same flow as stack tools (warn, prompt, skip/proceed) ## Considerations - ZSH_CUSTOM defaults to $ZSH/custom if not set - DISABLE_UPDATE_PROMPT=true for OMZ non-interactive - Some users may have customized themes/plugins - respect that ## Success Criteria - [ ] All shell tools update without prompts (in --yes mode) - [ ] Git-based updates handle conflicts gracefully - [ ] Atuin/Zoxide checksum verification works - [ ] Before/after versions logged - [ ] Skips missing components","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T13:26:10.463809-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T15:18:10.575757-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T15:18:10.575757-05:00","close_reason":"Implemented shell tool updates: omz, p10k, zsh plugins, atuin, zoxide","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-db0","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-75e","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T13:27:25.018434-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-dcy","title":"[codex.8] Audit web app for incorrect codex auth guidance","description":"## Goal Check apps/web/ for any incorrect guidance about codex-cli authentication. ## Files to Check Based on grep results: - apps/web/lib/jargon.ts - apps/web/app/wizard/run-installer/page.tsx - apps/web/lib/flywheel.ts - apps/web/app/workflow/page.tsx - apps/web/app/page.tsx ## What to Look For 1. Any mention of OPENAI_API_KEY for codex 2. Incorrect auth flow descriptions 3. Wizard steps that suggest API key setup ## Updates Needed 1. Replace API key guidance with OAuth flow description 2. Update any wizard steps about codex setup 3. Ensure jargon/definitions are correct ## Lower Priority Because The web app is less critical than the installer scripts - users who hit the web app will eventually run the installer which (after fixes) will give correct guidance. ## Acceptance Criteria 1. No incorrect OPENAI_API_KEY references for codex in web app 2. Correct OAuth flow described if mentioned","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:26:24.840054-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T16:45:56.25612-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T16:45:56.25612-05:00","close_reason":"Audited web app - no incorrect OPENAI_API_KEY references for codex found. Jargon entry describes Codex without auth details. CAAM references are about credential management (correct).","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-dcy","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-ua5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:26:36.94908-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-dfc","title":"Fix wizard layout heading causing mobile E2E failures","description":"Playwright mobile projects fail because WizardLayout renders a hidden (md:block)

step title before page content. Tests use page.locator('h1').first(), which resolves to the hidden layout heading on mobile. Also results in multiple h1s per page. Fix by removing or demoting layout heading, ensuring a single visible h1 per wizard page, and update tests if needed.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","assignee":"GreenDog","created_at":"2025-12-20T21:03:08.330927-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-20T21:30:35.67678-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-20T21:30:35.67678-05:00","close_reason":"Already fixed on main: apps/web/app/wizard/layout.tsx no longer renders a hidden

step title; only the step indicator remains, so mobile h1 selector mismatch is resolved.","labels":["a11y","ci","e2e","web"]} -{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-dmff","title":"Add headless browser auth flow explanation","description":"# Task: Add Headless Browser Auth Flow Explanation ## Parent Epic [EPIC] Authentication Flow for Headless VPS (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-3edj) ## Description When users run `claude` or `gh auth login` on the VPS, these tools try to open a browser. But there's no browser on the VPS! The tool prints a URL that must be opened on the user's laptop. This "device flow" is completely unexplained. ## Implementation Details Location: apps/web/app/wizard/status-check/page.tsx Add explanation before the auth commands: \`\`\`tsx

Your VPS doesn't have a web browser, so authentication works a bit differently:

  1. Run the login command (like claude)
  2. The terminal will display a URL (or code)
  3. Copy that URL and paste it in your laptop's browser
  4. Complete the login in your browser
  5. Return to your terminal — it should confirm success
If you see "Opening browser..." but nothing happens, that's normal! Just copy the URL shown and open it manually.
\`\`\` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Explains the device flow clearly - [ ] Step-by-step numbered list - [ ] Addresses "nothing happened" confusion - [ ] Appears before the auth command list - [ ] Works well on mobile ## UI/UX Notes - Use Laptop icon to reinforce "use your laptop's browser" - This should feel like a helpful heads-up, not a warning - Consider adding a visual diagram showing the flow","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:42:00.199364-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T14:25:01.154333-05:00"} +{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-dmff","title":"Add headless browser auth flow explanation","description":"# Task: Add Headless Browser Auth Flow Explanation ## Parent Epic [EPIC] Authentication Flow for Headless VPS (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-3edj) ## Description When users run `claude` or `gh auth login` on the VPS, these tools try to open a browser. But there's no browser on the VPS! The tool prints a URL that must be opened on the user's laptop. This "device flow" is completely unexplained. ## Implementation Details Location: apps/web/app/wizard/status-check/page.tsx Add explanation before the auth commands: \`\`\`tsx

Your VPS doesn't have a web browser, so authentication works a bit differently:

  1. Run the login command (like claude)
  2. The terminal will display a URL (or code)
  3. Copy that URL and paste it in your laptop's browser
  4. Complete the login in your browser
  5. Return to your terminal — it should confirm success
If you see "Opening browser..." but nothing happens, that's normal! Just copy the URL shown and open it manually.
\`\`\` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Explains the device flow clearly - [ ] Step-by-step numbered list - [ ] Addresses "nothing happened" confusion - [ ] Appears before the auth command list - [ ] Works well on mobile ## UI/UX Notes - Use Laptop icon to reinforce "use your laptop's browser" - This should feel like a helpful heads-up, not a warning - Consider adding a visual diagram showing the flow","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:42:00.199364-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T14:25:09.558506-05:00"} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-dvt","title":"EPIC: Comprehensive unit coverage (no mocks) + E2E logging","description":"# Goal Establish real-data, no-mock unit coverage and complete E2E integration scripts with detailed, human-readable logging. # Why - Current tests are partial (installer E2E + web Playwright). No full unit coverage across core Bash libs or manifest tooling. - E2E logs exist but are not standardized or granular enough for fast diagnosis. # Success Criteria - Unit coverage exists for manifest parser/generator, selection resolver, contract/security/preflight libs with real fixtures (no mocks/fakes). - E2E scripts cover bootstrap + selection + resume flows with structured logs and artifacts. - Tests are deterministic, diffable, and runnable locally + in CI. # Notes - "No mocks/fakes" means: use real manifests, real generated scripts, real flags; avoid stubbing core behaviors. - Logging should be timestamped, sectioned, and include command echo + exit codes. ","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:25:13.75871-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T17:16:59.6662-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T17:16:59.6662-05:00","close_reason":"All 6 children completed: coverage matrix, parser/generator tests (132 tests), installer lib tests (15 tests), test harness, selection E2E (26 tests), web E2E runner script.","labels":["qa","tests"]} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-dvt.1","title":"Define coverage matrix + real fixtures catalog","description":"# Scope Create a concrete coverage matrix that maps each core component to a test type, and standardize a catalog of **real** fixtures used by unit tests. # Deliverables - Coverage matrix table (component → test type → owner → status). - Fixture catalog list (real manifest YAMLs, generated scripts, seed state files) with storage location and usage notes. # Detailed comments - Components to include: `packages/manifest` (schema/parser/generator), `scripts/lib` (install_helpers, contract, security, preflight, logging), `install.sh` selection/introspection, `tests/vm` E2E. - Fixtures must be real (valid YAMLs and generated outputs), not synthetic mocks. - Ensure fixtures are minimal but realistic; avoid brittle snapshots. # Acceptance criteria - Matrix is stored in repo (likely `docs/tests/coverage_matrix.md`). - Fixture catalog references actual files committed in repo. ","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:25:21.951298-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T16:46:31.078442-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T16:46:31.078442-05:00","close_reason":"Added docs/tests/coverage_matrix.md and docs/tests/fixtures_catalog.md with real-file coverage matrix + fixture catalog","labels":["qa","tests"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-dvt.1","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-dvt","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:25:21.953283-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-dvt.2","title":"Unit tests: manifest parser/generator using real fixtures","description":"# Scope Add unit tests for `packages/manifest` (schema, parser, dependency resolution, generator outputs) using real YAML fixtures. # Detailed comments - Use real `acfs.manifest.yaml` plus a curated set of small, valid fixtures (no mocks). - Cover schema validation (new fields), dependency closure, cycle detection, and deterministic module ordering. - Validate generated `manifest_index.sh` content matches fixture expectations (IDs, phases, deps, defaults). # Acceptance criteria - Tests run via `bun test` (or existing harness) with real fixtures. - Failures are actionable (clear assertions and output). ","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:25:28.350421-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T16:58:31.870363-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T16:58:31.870363-05:00","close_reason":"Added comprehensive unit tests: schema.test.ts, parser.test.ts, generate.test.ts - 132 tests pass","labels":["manifest","tests"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-dvt.2","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-dvt","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:25:28.352603-05:00","created_by":"daemon"},{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-dvt.2","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-dvt.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:26:03.792033-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}

@@ -5,7 +5,7 @@ import { useRouter } from "next/navigation"; import { Key, ShieldCheck, ExternalLink } from "lucide-react"; import { Button } from "@/components/ui/button"; import { CommandCard } from "@/components/command-card"; -import { AlertCard, DetailsSection } from "@/components/alert-card"; +import { AlertCard, DetailsSection, OutputPreview } from "@/components/alert-card"; import { markStepComplete } from "@/lib/wizardSteps"; import { useWizardAnalytics } from "@/lib/hooks/useWizardAnalytics"; import { useUserOS } from "@/lib/userPreferences"; @@ -233,24 +233,65 @@ export default function GenerateSSHKeyPage() { - The terminal will ask you two questions. For both, just press - Enter without typing anything: -

- First prompt: "Enter passphrase" - → Just press Enter (leave it empty) -

- Second prompt: "Enter same passphrase again" - → Press Enter again -

- - Note: Not using a passphrase is fine for this purpose. It makes things simpler. - + The terminal will ask you a few questions. Here's exactly what you'll see: + +
+
+

First: File location

+ +

Enter file in which to save the key (/Users/you/.ssh/acfs_ed25519):

+ +

+ → Just press Enter! The path is already set by our command. +

+
+ +
+

Second: Passphrase

+ +

Enter passphrase (empty for no passphrase):

+ +

+ → Press Enter without typing anything. Leave it empty. +

+
+ +
+

Third: Confirm passphrase

+ +

Enter same passphrase again:

+ +

+ → Press Enter again. That's it! +

+
+
+ +

+ Note: Not using a passphrase is fine for this purpose. It keeps things simple. +

- You should see a message like "Your identification has been saved" - and some ASCII art that looks like a random pattern in a box. - This means your key was created! + When the key is created, you'll see a confirmation: +
+ +

Your identification has been saved in /Users/you/.ssh/acfs_ed25519

+

Your public key has been saved in /Users/you/.ssh/acfs_ed25519.pub

+

The key fingerprint is:

+

SHA256:xYz123abc... acfs

+

The key's randomart image is:

+ 
{`+--[ED25519 256]--+
+|     .o+*o.      |
+|    . o.=o+ .    |
+|     . =.= o     |
+|      o + = .    |
++----[SHA256]-----+`}
+ +
+

+ The randomart pattern means it worked! You now have SSH keys. +
